docs.text: BrowserAutomation BrowserAutomation provides Selenium-based browser automation for Fiona. It wraps Selenium WebDriver with the interface contracts defined in fiona.interfaces and exposes a thread-safe state machine, synchronous session wrappers for CLI integration, and a comprehensive error hierarchy. All Selenium dependencies are imported lazily so the package can be imported and inspected without Selenium installed. State Machine The BrowserManager uses a thread-safe state machine to track browser lifecycle: STOPPED ──→ STARTING ──→ RUNNING ↑ │ │ (no-op when running) │ │ │ └──── ERROR ──→ STARTING ───┘ States State Description STOPPED The browser is not running and has not been started. STARTING The browser process is being launched. RUNNING The browser is running and healthy. ERROR The browser has crashed or encountered a fatal error. Key Behaviors start() accepts {STOPPED, ERROR} as valid pre-states. On ERROR restart, stale _contexts and _instance are cleared before relaunch. start() is a no-op when already RUNNING — safe to call multiple times. There is no separate restart() method — calling start() from ERROR performs recovery directly. The DEGRADED state from the original Playwright design was removed as Selenium's simpler error model did not require it. from BrowserAutomation import get_browser_manager manager = get_browser_manager() manager.start() # STOPPED → STARTING → RUNNING manager.start() # no-op (already RUNNING) manager.stop() # RUNNING → STOPPED BrowserConfig Configuration is a frozen dataclass defined in fiona.interfaces : from fiona.interfaces import BrowserConfig Fields Field Type Default Description headless bool True Run without a visible UI window ("echo-off" mode). viewport_width int 1280 Default viewport width in pixels. viewport_height int 720 Default viewport height in pixels. data_dir str \| None None Persistent profile directory, or None for ephemeral. proxy str \| None None Proxy server URL, or None for direct connection. args tuple[str, ...] () Additional command-line arguments for the browser process. Default Configuration from BrowserAutomation._config import default_config, merge_config config = default_config(headless=True, proxy="http://proxy:8080") # Merge overrides on top of an existing config updated = merge_config(config, viewport_width=1920, viewport_height=1080) DEFAULT_HEADLESS = True in _config.py — the browser is invisible by default. All interaction happens through the FLoP UI ( fionaLocalPages/pages/browser.js ). IBrowserContext Interface Each isolated browser context has its own cookies, storage, and authentication — analogous to an incognito window. Contexts created from the same IBrowserInstance are fully isolated. from fiona.interfaces import IBrowserContext, NavigationEvent, NavigationResult Methods Method Description navigate(url, *, timeout=30.0, wait_until=NavigationEvent.LOAD) Navigate to a URL and return a NavigationResult . click(selector, *, timeout=5.0) Click the first element matching a CSS selector. type_text(selector, text, *, delay=0.01, timeout=5.0) Type text into an editable element with per-keystroke delay. get_text(selector, *, timeout=5.0) Retrieve the textContent of the first matching element. screenshot(*, path=None, full_page=False) Capture a PNG screenshot (returns base64-encoded bytes via the REST API). evaluate(js, *, timeout=5.0) Execute JavaScript in the page's main frame. close() Close the context and release resources. Properties Property Type Description is_closed bool True after close() has been called. context_id str Stable unique identifier for this context. NavigationEvent Controls how long navigate() waits before considering the navigation complete: Value Description NavigationEvent.LOAD Wait for the full load event (all resources fetched). NavigationEvent.DOM_CONTENT Wait only until the DOM is ready (faster, but styles/fonts may be missing). NavigationEvent.NETWORK_IDLE Wait until no network requests have been made for ~500 ms. SeleniumBrowserProvider SeleniumBrowserProvider is the concrete implementation of IBrowserProvider . It translates BrowserConfig into Selenium WebDriver launch arguments and maps Selenium/WebDriver exceptions into the Fiona error hierarchy. Chrome Binary Resolution Chrome is resolved in the following order (see _selenium_provider.py ): FIONA_CHROME_BINARY environment variable — explicit override google-chrome-stable on PATH — system-installed Chrome Playwright-downloaded Chromium at ~/.cache/ms-playwright/chromium-1223/chrome-linux64/chrome — fallback for systems where Playwright was previously installed This solves the common webdriver-manager version mismatch (e.g., ChromeDriver v140 vs Playwright Chrome v148). Lazy Imports Selenium is imported inside methods , not at module level: def launch(self, config: BrowserConfig) -> IBrowserInstance: from selenium import webdriver # lazy import from selenium.webdriver.chrome.options import Options options = Options() options.binary_location = _resolve_chrome_binary() options.headless = config.headless driver = webdriver.Chrome(options=options) return SeleniumBrowserInstance(driver=driver) If Selenium is not installed, a helpful RuntimeError is raised: RuntimeError: Selenium is not installed. Install it with: pip install selenium webdriver-manager Capabilities The provider advertises these capabilities: SELENIUM_CAPABILITIES = frozenset({ "screenshot", "js_eval", "multi_context", "emulation", }) Note: pdf and network_intercept capabilities (available in Playwright) are not supported by Selenium WebDriver. If needed, these can be added via browser DevTools Protocol (CDP) bindings. Session Management SessionManager SessionManager provides synchronous wrappers around the async BrowserManager for use in CLI handlers and agent commands. It owns a dedicated event loop running in a daemon thread. from BrowserAutomation._session_manager import SessionManager session = SessionManager() session.start() # Launches event loop thread + browser session.navigate("https://example.com") session.click("button#submit") session.type_text("#search", "query") text = session.get_text(".result") data = session.screenshot(path="/tmp/page.png") js_result = session.evaluate("document.title") session.stop() Module-Level Convenience API The __init__.py exports convenience functions that operate on a module-level default BrowserManager : from BrowserAutomation import ( browser_status, create_context, navigate, click_element, type_text, get_text_content, capture_screenshot, evaluate_script, get_browser_manager, ) These are thin wrappers around a shared singleton manager created on first access: manager = get_browser_manager() manager.start() status = browser_status() # BrowserManagerState.RUNNING navigate("https://example.com") click_element("#go") type_text("#input", "hello") capture_screenshot(path="shot.png") Error Hierarchy All browser errors are defined in fiona.interfaces and re-exported from BrowserAutomation . BrowserError ├── BrowserLaunchError — Browser process could not be started ├── BrowserNotRunning — Operation requires a running browser ├── BrowserShutdownError — Failed to shut down the browser cleanly ├── BrowserTimeout — Base timeout error │ ├── NavigationTimeout — Page navigation exceeded timeout │ └── SelectorTimeout — Waiting for a DOM selector timed out ├── ElementNotFound — DOM element does not exist on the page ├── ElementNotInteractable — Element found but cannot be interacted with ├── ScriptExecutionError — JavaScript evaluation failed └── BrowserCrashError — Browser process terminated unexpectedly from BrowserAutomation import ( BrowserError, BrowserLaunchError, BrowserNotRunning, BrowserShutdownError, BrowserTimeout, NavigationTimeout, SelectorTimeout, ElementNotFound, ElementNotInteractable, ScriptExecutionError, BrowserCrashError, ) try: manager.start() except BrowserLaunchError as exc: print(f"Failed to launch: {exc}") Deprecated: PlaywrightProvider The original PlaywrightBrowserProvider ( _playwright_provider.py ) has been replaced by SeleniumBrowserProvider . The old file is preserved as dead code for reference but has no active imports . Its test file ( tests/browser/test_playwright_provider.py ) is entirely skipped via @pytest.mark.skip . CLI Commands Browser automation is accessible via the fiona browser command (aliased as fiona br ). fiona browser start # Start the browser engine fiona browser stop # Stop the browser engine fiona browser status # Show browser status as JSON fiona browser navigate <url> # Navigate to a URL fiona browser click <sel> # Click a CSS selector fiona browser type <sel> txt # Type text into an element fiona browser screenshot # Capture a screenshot Command Details fiona browser start fiona browser start Starts the browser. Safe to call when already running (no-op). fiona browser stop fiona browser stop Stops the browser gracefully. Idempotent. fiona browser status fiona browser status Outputs structured JSON: { "state": "running", "config": { "headless": true, "viewport_width": 1280, "viewport_height": 720 } } fiona browser navigate fiona browser navigate <url> [--timeout 30.0] Arguments: Argument Type Default Description url positional — URL to navigate to. --timeout float 30.0 Navigation timeout in seconds. Outputs structured JSON with the navigation result: { "url": "https://example.com/", "status_code": 200, "title": "Example Domain", "duration_ms": 1234.56 } fiona browser click fiona browser click <selector> [--timeout 5.0] Arguments: Argument Type Default Description selector positional — CSS selector to click. --timeout float 5.0 Timeout in seconds. fiona browser type fiona browser type <selector> <text> [--delay 0.01] [--timeout 5.0] Arguments: Argument Type Default Description selector positional — CSS selector for the target element. text positional — Text to type. --delay float 0.01 Delay between keystrokes (seconds). --timeout float 5.0 Timeout in seconds. fiona browser screenshot fiona browser screenshot [--output path] Arguments: Argument Type Default Description --output Path stdout Output file path. If omitted, raw PNG bytes are written to stdout. Note: --full-page is not supported by the Selenium provider (Playwright-only feature). Alias The entire browser command group is aliased as br : fiona br start fiona br status fiona br navigate https://example.com Dependencies Selenium is a required dependency of Fiona. Install it with: pip install fiona[browser] # or directly: pip install selenium>=4.20.0 webdriver-manager>=4.0.0 The minimum required versions are selenium>=4.20.0 and webdriver-manager>=4.0.0 , declared under the [browser] optional-dependencies group in pyproject.toml . webdriver-manager automatically handles ChromeDriver binary management — no manual chromedriver installation needed. A Chrome/Chromium browser must be available on the system PATH or pointed to via the FIONA_CHROME_BINARY environment variable.

docs.text: CamComs CamComs is Fiona's communications layer. Responsibility encrypted communications decryption/encryption remote device connectivity command/session transport host receiver and service behavior Current Direction ESP32 sender -> encoded encrypted HTTP POST -> Fiona host receiver The host decodes and decrypts messages with its CamComs private identity. Current Implementation CamComs provides: CamComsIdentity.generate() for encryption and signing keypairs public key bundles that are safe to share X25519 key agreement HKDF-SHA256 key derivation AES-GCM encrypted payloads Ed25519 sender signatures base64url JSON envelope encoding/decoding trusted sender public-key storage replay protection for duplicate/stale messages host receiver and host service skeleton audit log for accepted/rejected message processing strict JSON instruction validation optional passphrase encryption for private key JSON Default Storage Paths ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/host.private.json ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/host.public.json ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/esp32.private.json ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/esp32.public.json ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/trusted/ ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/audit.log Useful Commands python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms smoke-test python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms paths python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms keygen --device-id host python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms keygen --device-id esp32 python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms trust --public ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/esp32.public.json python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms trust --list python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms audit Key Lifecycle Generate host keys: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms keygen --device-id host Generate ESP32 provisioning keys: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms keygen --device-id esp32 Trust a sender: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms trust --public ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/esp32.public.json List trusted senders: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms trust --list Remove a sender: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms trust --remove esp32 Encrypt / Decrypt Encrypt a press instruction: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms encrypt \ --sender-private ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/esp32.private.json \ --recipient-public ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/host.public.json \ --press alt s Encrypt structured instruction JSON: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms encrypt \ --sender-private ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/esp32.private.json \ --recipient-public ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/host.public.json \ --instruction-json '{"version":1,"type":"press","keys":["alt","s"]}' Decrypt an encoded envelope: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms decrypt \ --recipient-private ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/host.private.json \ --sender-public ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/esp32.public.json \ --encoded '<encoded-message>' Transport Send an encoded message to a host/IP endpoint: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms send \ --host 192.168.1.50 \ --port 8080 \ --path / \ --encoded '<encoded-message>' Receiver Run the receiver directly: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms receive --private ~/.config/fiona/camcoms/host.private.json --port 8080 Receiver options: --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8081 --execute to execute approved QuikTieper remote actions instead of dry-run --trusted-dir <dir> to use a custom trusted sender directory Host Service python3 -m fiona.cli host init python3 -m fiona.cli host status python3 -m fiona.cli host run python3 -m fiona.cli host install-service --print Nested service commands also exist: python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms service init python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms service status python3 -m fiona.cli camcoms service run

docs.text: CmdTrace CmdTrace provides a high-performance observability layer for Fiona. It records every action routed through the central ActionRouter into an append-only JSONL log and exposes analytics, search, export, compaction, and live-tailing. Purpose Audit Trail : Immutable record of every command executed via Fiona CLI, GUI, or remote ESP32 instructions. Performance Monitoring : Records execution duration of every action for latency analysis. Failure Analysis : Logs success/failure status and error messages for every routed task. Operations : Statistics, advanced search, export to JSON/CSV, auto-compaction by size or age, and real-time tail monitoring. Commands Statistics fiona action stats Returns 14 fields: total/success/failure/unknown event counts, per-action breakdown, duration statistics (total/avg/max), time range, file size. View recent history fiona action history --limit 20 Filter history by action name fiona action history --name host.status Advanced search fiona action search --query "timeout" # Full-text search across all event fields fiona action search --status ok # Only successful actions fiona action search --status fail # Only failed actions fiona action search --regex "host\.\w+" # Python regex match against serialized JSON fiona action search --start-time 2026-01-01T00:00:00 --end-time 2026-06-30T23:59:59 fiona action search --limit 100 Export fiona action export --format json # Pretty-printed JSON array fiona action export --format csv # CSV with auto-discovered columns fiona action export --output /path/to/export.json Compact / Rotate fiona action compact # Default: 10 MB max, 30 days, keep last 10k fiona action compact --max-size-mb 5 --max-age-days 7 --keep-last 1000 Returns a report: original/new size, original/new entry count, how many trimmed by size vs age. Clear the trace log fiona action clear Live tail (follow new entries) fiona action tail # Follow new entries in real-time fiona action tail --interval 0.5 # Poll every 0.5 seconds API Endpoints Method Endpoint Description GET /api/v1/actions/history Recent trace events (query: limit , name ) GET /api/v1/actions/stats Trace analytics (14-field statistics object) TUI Integration The History page in the fiona cli TUI provides a real-time sliding view of the latest system activity using CmdTrace.read_trace() . Storage Data is stored as JSON Lines (one JSON object per line) in: ~/.config/fiona/cmdtrace.jsonl Auto-compaction When trace_compact() is called (or configured to run periodically), the file is checked against size and age thresholds. Entries beyond the configured limits are trimmed, with the most recent keep_last entries preserved. Python API from CmdTrace import ( append_trace, # Append an event to the trace read_trace, # Read recent events (filter by action_name, limit) clear_trace, # Delete the trace file trace_stats, # Get 14-field analytics dictionary trace_search, # Advanced search (query, status, regex, time range, limit) trace_export, # Export trace to JSON or CSV file trace_compact, # Compact/rotate by file size or entry age trace_tail, # Iterator that yields new events as they're written ) trace_stats(path) -> dict Returns: - total_events , skipped_lines — event counts - per_action — {action_name: count} sorted by frequency - success_count , failure_count , unknown_status_count - first_event_time , last_event_time , time_span_seconds - total_duration , avg_duration , max_duration - file_size_bytes , file_size_human trace_search(*, query, action_name, status, start_time, end_time, regex, limit, path) -> list[dict] All parameters are keyword-only: - query — case-insensitive substring search across ALL string values - action_name — exact match on event["action"] - status — True for ok, False for failures, None for no filter - start_time / end_time — ISO timestamp range filter - regex — Python re.search against json.dumps(event) - limit — max results (default 100) trace_export(path, output, fmt) -> Path fmt="json" — write as pretty-printed JSON array fmt="csv" — write as CSV with auto-discovered headers If output is None, generates cmdtrace-export-{timestamp}.{fmt} trace_compact(*, max_size_mb, max_age_days, keep_last, path) -> dict Returns compaction report with original_size_bytes , new_size_bytes , original_entries , new_entries , trimmed_by_size , trimmed_by_age . trace_tail(path, interval) -> Iterator[dict] Returns an iterator that polls for file growth every interval seconds. Handles file truncation gracefully. Call .close() to stop.

docs.text: DataClient DataClient is Fiona's standalone research and data collection app. It is not part of the shared fiona edit GUI. Open The App python3 -m fiona.cli dataclient If Fiona is installed into the active environment: fiona dataclient GUI Areas The GUI has two main tabs: Research : quick topic mining and bounded deep research. MiniExcel : lightweight CSV/JSON/SQLite viewer and editor. The app menu includes a Miner menu for starting quick mining, starting deep research, and clearing the miner log without switching tabs. Research Mode Quick mode: searches DuckDuckGo HTML results scrapes the selected number of pages summarizes page text saves the results as CSV Deep mode: starts from search result seed pages follows same-domain links by default obeys depth and page-limit controls records page depth and parent URL CLI quick mining: python3 -m fiona.cli dataclient mine "local desktop automation" --out ./research.csv --max-links 30 CLI deep research: python3 -m fiona.cli dataclient deep "local desktop automation" --out ./deep-research.csv --seed-links 10 --depth 1 --page-limit 50 Deep mode is intentionally bounded. Use --cross-domain only when broader crawling is intentional. Output Columns DataClient research exports use these columns: topic url title summary depth parent_url MiniExcel MiniExcel can: open CSV, JSON, and SQLite tables display data in rows and columns edit the selected cell add rows add columns delete rows save or export the table use a formula bar for selected cells Formula bar examples: =A1 =B1:B5 =SUM(B1:B5) =AVG(C1:C10) =LOWER(A1) Supported safe functions include SUM , AVG , MIN , MAX , COUNT , LEN , LOWER , and UPPER . Convert And Preview Convert between table formats: python3 -m fiona.cli dataclient convert ./research.csv --out ./research.json python3 -m fiona.cli dataclient convert ./research.json --out ./research.db --table research Preview a table from the terminal: python3 -m fiona.cli dataclient view ./research.csv --limit 5

docs.text: ESP32 Payload The CamComs/esp32payload/ folder is the ESP32-side sender payload template. Current Direction ESP32 is the sender. Host is the receiver. ESP32 encrypts an instruction/event for the host public key. ESP32 POSTs the encoded envelope to the host IP endpoint. Host decodes and decrypts with its CamComs private identity. Envelope Requirements The ESP32 envelope must match CamComs.encryption : X25519 ephemeral key agreement against the host encryption public key HKDF-SHA256 message key derivation AES-GCM encrypted payload Ed25519 signature from the ESP32 signing key base64url JSON envelope encoded as ASCII for transport Host Setup Generate the host receiver identity: fiona camcoms keygen --device-id host --private-out host.private.json --public-out host.public.json Generate the ESP32 sender identity on the host for provisioning: fiona camcoms keygen --device-id esp32 --private-out esp32.private.json --public-out esp32.public.json Copy these values into esp32payload.ino : HOST_DEVICE_ID from host.public.json HOST_ENCRYPTION_PUBLIC_KEY_B64 from host.public.json ESP32_DEVICE_ID from esp32.private.json ESP32_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY_B64 from esp32.private.json The host should use esp32.public.json as the expected sender when decrypting. Firmware Notes esp32payload.ino is intentionally a firmware template. Wi-Fi/HTTP and envelope construction are concrete, while the tiny crypto adapter section is isolated because the exact ESP32 crypto library choice may change. For the current one-way direction, the ESP32 only needs: host encryption public key ESP32 signing private key The ESP32 static encryption private key is not needed until the host also encrypts responses back to the ESP32.

docs.text: EyeControl EyeControl is Fiona's optional camera-based eye-controlled mouse tracker. The tracker now lives at EyeControl/Eye_Controlled_Mouse_Tracker.py and is wrapped as a package so normal Fiona imports, CLI help, docs builds, and tests do not require camera access. Status python3 -m fiona.cli eyecontrol status The status command checks optional dependencies without opening a camera: requests , cv2 , numpy , mediapipe , and pyautogui . Install Optional Dependencies pip install -e ".[eyecontrol]" Run IP camera snapshot URL: python3 -m fiona.cli eyecontrol run --url http://192.168.0.103:8080/shot.jpg Local OpenCV camera: python3 -m fiona.cli eyecontrol run --camera-index 0 Safer pointer-only test mode: python3 -m fiona.cli eyecontrol run --camera-index 0 --no-click Runtime Mechanics camera frame -> OpenCV decode/capture -> horizontal flip -> RGB conversion -> MediaPipe face mesh -> iris landmark tracking -> screen coordinate mapping -> PyAutoGUI pointer move -> optional blink-click detection -> OpenCV preview window The camera loop exits when the OpenCV preview window receives Esc . Current Limits requires a real camera or IP camera feed requires optional vision/input packages not yet integrated into the shared GUI not yet connected to SeeOnDesk context or QuikTieper permissions blink-clicking should be tested with --no-click first

docs.text: FionaCore FionaCore provides shared primitives used across all Fiona subsystems: action routing, security policies, macro execution, shell safety, verification prompts, notifications, speech synthesis, approval management, permissions, and voice integration. Action Router The central action dispatch system. Routes named actions to registered handler functions with ACL checking, verification prompts, and trace logging. from FionaCore.actions import ActionRouter, ActionResult router = ActionRouter() router.register("host.status", lambda: {"ok": True}) result = router.run("host.status") Key features: ACL integration : Sender-scoped permission rules checked before action execution Verification prompts : High-risk actions require user confirmation Thread safety : Uses threading.RLock for concurrent access Trace logging : Every action result is recorded in the trace ACL (Access Control) Sender-scoped permission system. See the Security page for full details. from FionaCore.acl import SenderACLRule, resolve_sender_profile, resolve_sender_scope Rules match sender IDs using fnmatch patterns Built-in profiles: local , ssh , websocket , ble Default tiers: local → agent:* → any other sender Unknown senders are denied by default Shell Safety Blocks destructive shell commands across all 5 shell execution points in Fiona. See the Security page for the full pattern list. from FionaCore.shell_safety import safe_os_system, safe_popen_shell, check_command_safety Protected commands: rm -rf / , mkfs , dd , wipefs , chmod 777 /etc/shadow , curl | bash , and 25+ other destructive patterns. Wrapped execution points: fiona/cli.py ( run-shell command) TerminalAssist/gui.py (shell action execution) TerminalAssist/tui.py (shell action execution) QuikTieper/launcher.py (app launch shell) QuikTieper/remote.py (remote action shell) Macro Engine Extended macro execution engine with waits, conditions, branching, and variable interpolation. MacroStep from FionaCore.macros import MacroStep step = MacroStep( action="launch:brave", wait_type="wait_for_window", wait_value="Brave", condition_type="process_running", condition_value="brave", fallback_action="notify:Brave is running", ) Wait Types Type Behavior sleep Pause for N milliseconds wait_for_window Poll active window title/class up to 30s wait_for_process Poll /proc for process name up to 30s Condition Types Type Behavior window_active Check if window title/class matches process_running Check if process name found in /proc action_result Check previous step result ( action_name:ok / action_name:failed ) Variable Interpolation String fields support ${variable_name} placeholders: step = MacroStep(action="notify:Hello ${user_name}") Available variables: all entries in the execution context, plus step_index , last_result , and last_ok . GOTO Steps with action GOTO:<macro_name> branch to another macro's steps (max depth 10 to prevent circular references). CLI fiona --run-macro my_macro # Execute a named macro fiona --list-macros # List all macros and step counts Verification Prompts Requires user confirmation for high-risk actions before execution. from FionaCore.verification import StdoutVerificationPrompt, DesktopVerificationPrompt Desktop prompt : Tkinter dialog with Yes/No buttons Stdout prompt : Terminal-based prompt with timeout Falls back from desktop → terminal → automatic denial Notifications Desktop notification builder with optional speech output. from FionaCore.notifications import build_notification build_notification("Fiona", "Task complete", urgency="normal") Uses notify-send for desktop notifications Truncates long bodies automatically Optional speech integration via FionaCore.speech Speech Minimal speech synthesis via system TTS engines. from FionaCore.speech import speak speak("Task complete") # Uses espeak or festival Prefers espeak (fast, widely available) Falls back to festival Silent if no TTS engine is installed ApprovalManager Human-in-the-loop plan approval system used by the Agent orchestrator. Agent threads submit plans and block on wait_for_approval() while server threads call approve_plan() / deny_plan() in response to human action. PlanStatus from FionaCore.approval import PlanStatus Status Description PENDING Awaiting human approval APPROVED Human approved, ready to execute DENIED Human denied EXECUTING Currently being executed by agent COMPLETED All steps done FAILED Execution failed CANCELLED Human cancelled during execution PlannedStep from FionaCore.approval import PlannedStep step = PlannedStep( step_number=1, action="browser_navigate", params={"url": "https://example.com"}, reasoning="Navigate to the target URL", risk="medium", requires_approval=False, ) ApprovalManager API from FionaCore.approval import ApprovalManager, get_approval_manager manager = get_approval_manager() # Module-level singleton # Agent side: submit a plan and wait for human decision plan_id = manager.submit_plan( goal="Open the browser to example.com", steps=[step], agent_id="fiona-agent", ) status = manager.wait_for_approval(plan_id, timeout=300) # 'approved' | 'denied' | 'timeout' # Server/GUI side: approve or deny manager.approve_plan(plan_id) manager.deny_plan(plan_id, reason="Not now") # Lifecycle management manager.mark_executing(plan_id) manager.mark_completed(plan_id, summary="All steps done") manager.mark_failed(plan_id, error="Navigation timed out") manager.cancel_plan(plan_id, reason="User interrupted") The on_change callback notifies subscribers when any plan status changes: def on_plan_change(plan_id: str) -> None: plan = manager.get_plan(plan_id) print(f"Plan {plan_id} is now {plan['status']}") manager.on_change(on_plan_change) Permissions Two permission systems work together to control what agents and senders can do. PermissionProfile FionaCore.permissions defines risk-gated permission profiles. from FionaCore.permissions import PermissionProfile, permission_allows, permission_profile Built-in profiles: Profile Max Risk Permissions local high read, control, service, network, gui agent medium read, control, network remote_safe low read # Check if a given profile allows an operation allowed = permission_allows( profile="agent", risk="medium", permission="network", ) PermissionEnforcer Agent.permission provides PermissionEnforcer and SafeActionRouter for runtime tool-access gating within the Agent subsystem. The enforcer checks whether a specific tool name is permitted by the active personality, and SafeActionRouter wraps FionaCore.ActionRouter with these checks. from Agent.permission import PermissionEnforcer, SafeActionRouter enforcer = PermissionEnforcer(personality) enforcer.assert_tool_allowed("browser_navigate") # Raises AgentPermissionError if denied safe_router = SafeActionRouter(enforcer=enforcer, router=action_router) result = safe_router.run("browser_navigate", url="https://example.com") Key points: PermissionEnforcer is optional — backward compatibility is preserved. SafeActionRouter.run() checks permission before delegating to ActionRouter.run() . SafeActionRouter.run_with_fallback() attempts CLI fallback if the action is not registered. list_allowed_actions() returns the intersection of registered actions and the personality's allowed tools. Voice Integration Voice command parsing and speech-to-text transcription. VoiceCommand from FionaCore.voice import VoiceCommand, parse_voice_command parse_voice_command() matches spoken text against registered patterns and returns a VoiceCommand with the recognized action: cmd = parse_voice_command("Show host status") # VoiceCommand(text="Show host status", action="host.status", confidence=0.9) cmd = parse_voice_command("What is open on the desktop?") # VoiceCommand(text="What is open on the desktop?", action="seeondesk.status", confidence=0.9) Supported voice commands: Speech Pattern Mapped Action "show/open the host status" host.status "show/open the fat" / "terminal dashboard" fat.status "camcoms smoke/test" / "encryption test" camcoms.smoke "show/open camcoms paths" camcoms.paths "show/list bindings/shortcuts" quiktieper.list "desktop status" / "what is open" seeondesk.status "eye control status" eyecontrol.status "agent status" / "lm studio status" agent.status WhisperEngine from FionaCore.voice_engine import WhisperEngine, quick_transcribe WhisperEngine wraps faster-whisper for local speech-to-text transcription: engine = WhisperEngine(model_size="tiny", device="cpu", compute_type="int8") # Transcribe from a numpy audio buffer text = engine.transcribe_audio_buffer(audio_data, sample_rate=16000) # Record and transcribe in one call text = engine.listen_and_transcribe(duration_seconds=5.0) quick_transcribe() provides a one-shot convenience wrapper: from FionaCore.voice_engine import quick_transcribe text = quick_transcribe(phrase_seconds=3.0) # Records and returns transcribed text Optional dependencies: faster-whisper , sounddevice , numpy .

docs.text: from FionaCore.macros import MacroStep step = MacroStep( action="launch:brave", wait_type="wait_for_window", wait_value="Brave", condition_type="process_running", condition_value="brave", fallback_action="notify:Brave is running", )
docs.title: MacroStep

docs.text: Type Behavior sleep Pause for N milliseconds wait_for_window Poll active window title/class up to 30s wait_for_process Poll /proc for process name up to 30s
docs.title: Wait Types

docs.text: Type Behavior window_active Check if window title/class matches process_running Check if process name found in /proc action_result Check previous step result ( action_name:ok / action_name:failed )
docs.title: Condition Types

docs.text: String fields support ${variable_name} placeholders: step = MacroStep(action="notify:Hello ${user_name}") Available variables: all entries in the execution context, plus step_index , last_result , and last_ok .
docs.title: Variable Interpolation

docs.text: Steps with action GOTO:<macro_name> branch to another macro's steps (max depth 10 to prevent circular references).
docs.title: GOTO

docs.text: fiona --run-macro my_macro # Execute a named macro fiona --list-macros # List all macros and step counts
docs.title: CLI

docs.text: Requires user confirmation for high-risk actions before execution. from FionaCore.verification import StdoutVerificationPrompt, DesktopVerificationPrompt Desktop prompt : Tkinter dialog with Yes/No buttons Stdout prompt : Terminal-based prompt with timeout Falls back from desktop → terminal → automatic denial
docs.title: Verification Prompts

docs.text: Desktop notification builder with optional speech output. from FionaCore.notifications import build_notification build_notification("Fiona", "Task complete", urgency="normal") Uses notify-send for desktop notifications Truncates long bodies automatically Optional speech integration via FionaCore.speech
docs.title: Notifications

docs.text: Minimal speech synthesis via system TTS engines. from FionaCore.speech import speak speak("Task complete") # Uses espeak or festival Prefers espeak (fast, widely available) Falls back to festival Silent if no TTS engine is installed

docs.text: Human-in-the-loop plan approval system used by the Agent orchestrator. Agent threads submit plans and block on wait_for_approval() while server threads call approve_plan() / deny_plan() in response to human action.
docs.title: ApprovalManager

docs.text: from FionaCore.approval import PlanStatus Status Description PENDING Awaiting human approval APPROVED Human approved, ready to execute DENIED Human denied EXECUTING Currently being executed by agent COMPLETED All steps done FAILED Execution failed CANCELLED Human cancelled during execution
docs.title: PlanStatus

docs.text: from FionaCore.approval import PlannedStep step = PlannedStep( step_number=1, action="browser_navigate", params={"url": "https://example.com"}, reasoning="Navigate to the target URL", risk="medium", requires_approval=False, )
docs.title: PlannedStep

docs.text: from FionaCore.approval import ApprovalManager, get_approval_manager manager = get_approval_manager() # Module-level singleton # Agent side: submit a plan and wait for human decision plan_id = manager.submit_plan( goal="Open the browser to example.com", steps=[step], agent_id="fiona-agent", ) status = manager.wait_for_approval(plan_id, timeout=300) # 'approved' | 'denied' | 'timeout' # Server/GUI side: approve or deny manager.approve_plan(plan_id) manager.deny_plan(plan_id, reason="Not now") # Lifecycle management manager.mark_executing(plan_id) manager.mark_completed(plan_id, summary="All steps done") manager.mark_failed(plan_id, error="Navigation timed out") manager.cancel_plan(plan_id, reason="User interrupted") The on_change callback notifies subscribers when any plan status changes: def on_plan_change(plan_id: str) -> None: plan = manager.get_plan(plan_id) print(f"Plan {plan_id} is now {plan['status']}") manager.on_change(on_plan_change)
docs.title: ApprovalManager API

docs.text: Two permission systems work together to control what agents and senders can do.
docs.title: Permissions

docs.text: FionaCore.permissions defines risk-gated permission profiles. from FionaCore.permissions import PermissionProfile, permission_allows, permission_profile Built-in profiles: Profile Max Risk Permissions local high read, control, service, network, gui agent medium read, control, network remote_safe low read # Check if a given profile allows an operation allowed = permission_allows( profile="agent", risk="medium", permission="network", )
docs.title: PermissionProfile

docs.text: Agent.permission provides PermissionEnforcer and SafeActionRouter for runtime tool-access gating within the Agent subsystem. The enforcer checks whether a specific tool name is permitted by the active personality, and SafeActionRouter wraps FionaCore.ActionRouter with these checks. from Agent.permission import PermissionEnforcer, SafeActionRouter enforcer = PermissionEnforcer(personality) enforcer.assert_tool_allowed("browser_navigate") # Raises AgentPermissionError if denied safe_router = SafeActionRouter(enforcer=enforcer, router=action_router) result = safe_router.run("browser_navigate", url="https://example.com") Key points: PermissionEnforcer is optional — backward compatibility is preserved. SafeActionRouter.run() checks permission before delegating to ActionRouter.run() . SafeActionRouter.run_with_fallback() attempts CLI fallback if the action is not registered. list_allowed_actions() returns the intersection of registered actions and the personality's allowed tools.
docs.title: PermissionEnforcer

docs.text: Voice command parsing and speech-to-text transcription.
docs.title: Voice Integration

docs.text: from FionaCore.voice import VoiceCommand, parse_voice_command parse_voice_command() matches spoken text against registered patterns and returns a VoiceCommand with the recognized action: cmd = parse_voice_command("Show host status") # VoiceCommand(text="Show host status", action="host.status", confidence=0.9) cmd = parse_voice_command("What is open on the desktop?") # VoiceCommand(text="What is open on the desktop?", action="seeondesk.status", confidence=0.9) Supported voice commands: Speech Pattern Mapped Action "show/open the host status" host.status "show/open the fat" / "terminal dashboard" fat.status "camcoms smoke/test" / "encryption test" camcoms.smoke "show/open camcoms paths" camcoms.paths "show/list bindings/shortcuts" quiktieper.list "desktop status" / "what is open" seeondesk.status "eye control status" eyecontrol.status "agent status" / "lm studio status" agent.status
docs.title: VoiceCommand

docs.text: from FionaCore.voice_engine import WhisperEngine, quick_transcribe WhisperEngine wraps faster-whisper for local speech-to-text transcription: engine = WhisperEngine(model_size="tiny", device="cpu", compute_type="int8") # Transcribe from a numpy audio buffer text = engine.transcribe_audio_buffer(audio_data, sample_rate=16000) # Record and transcribe in one call text = engine.listen_and_transcribe(duration_seconds=5.0) quick_transcribe() provides a one-shot convenience wrapper: from FionaCore.voice_engine import quick_transcribe text = quick_transcribe(phrase_seconds=3.0) # Records and returns transcribed text Optional dependencies: faster-whisper , sounddevice , numpy .
docs.title: WhisperEngine

docs.text: fionaLocalPages — Web Dashboard fionaLocalPages ( fionaLocalPages/ ) is a browser-based operational dashboard for Fiona. It serves as a modern SPA control surface alongside the existing CLI and Tkinter GUI. Architecture Python backend (aiohttp) → HTML templates → JS modules (thin SPA layer) Backend — server/app.py Python aiohttp HTTP server with 24 handler modules and 115+ REST API endpoints at /api/v1/ . Also serves WebSocket at /ws for real-time push and SSE at /api/v1/stream . Handler modules: Module Endpoints Purpose agents_crud.py 7 Agent CRUD (list, create, pause, resume, stop, restart) + Ollama model check browser.py 7+ Selenium browser automation (start, stop, status, navigate, click, type, screenshot) terminal.py 4 Terminal execution + autocomplete + cwd (tracked server-side, every response includes "cwd" ) quiktieper.py 8 QuikTieper status, presets, desktop-app discovery, import-apps, assign-keys, launcher control settings_handler.py 2 Read/write ~/.config/fiona/settings.txt (JSON) bindings.py 3 Key binding list, save, app discovery notifications_handler.py 3 Notification feed (list, create, dismiss) phiconnect.py 5 PhiConnect status, identity, messages, send, trust actions.py 3 Action list, get, run agent.py 5 Chat sessions CRUD + message streaming (SSE) system.py 2 Health check, capabilities voice.py 2 Voice status, command execution files.py 3 File browser (list, read, write) config.py 2 Configuration get/update desktop.py 1 SeeOnDesk desktop status recall.py 4 RecallVault search, remember, forget, categories macros.py 5 Macro CRUD + run camcoms.py 4 CamComs status, encrypt, decrypt, send tasks.py 1 Task list plugins.py 1 Plugin list tools_handler.py 2 Tool list, execute sciretrieval.py 1 Scientific knowledge retrieval camcoms.py 4 Encryption/decryption service status Frontend — Pages + Templates 27 page modules, each following the createPage(routeInfo) → { render, mount, destroy } pattern: render() returns a mount-point <div> only mount() is async — calls await loadTemplate('name', data) to fetch and inject HTML from templates/{name}.html , then binds events destroy() cleans up intervals, listeners, and subscriptions Template system ( js/template-loader.js ): - {{variable}} — escaped text interpolation - {{{rawHtml}}} — raw HTML (used for SVG icons) - {{#if}} / {{#each}} — conditionals and loops The router also handles plain object exports ( { render, mount, destroy } ) — this was a critical fix for blank pages when browser.js , terminal.js , and file-explorer.js exported objects instead of factory functions. All 27 routes: # Route Page Backend Features 1 / dashboard.js system Live metrics, agent status, activity feed 2 /chat chat.js agent AI chat with streaming, model selection 3 /agents agents.js agents_crud Agent list + qwen3:8b indicator 4 /agents/:id agent-status.js agent Tabbed agent detail 5 /actions actions.js actions Action list + history, search, dry-run 6 /bindings bindings.js bindings Two-panel key binding editor 7 /phiconnect phiconnect.js phiconnect Secure messaging status + send 8 /macros macros.js macros Macro cards with step preview, run/dry-run 9 /terminal terminal.js terminal Interactive terminal (backend-driven, cwd tracked server-side) 10 /notifications notifications.js notifications Urgency-coded list with dismiss 11 /settings settings.js config Multi-tab settings panels 12 /performance performance.js system Real-time CPU/memory Canvas 2D charts 13 /files file-explorer.js files File tree browser 14 /browser browser.js browser Selenium automation controls (headless by default) 15 /tasks tasks.js — Kanban task board 16 /plugins plugins.js — Plugin manager (headless: true, Selenium-based) 17 /logs logs.js — Log viewer 18 /config config.js config Config file editor 19 /diagnostics diagnostics.js — Health check grid 20 /devtools devtools.js — Developer tools panel 21 /workspace workspace.js — Project workspace overview 22 /camcoms camcoms.js camcoms Service status + fingerprint 23 /recall recall.js recall Key-value memory search 24 /desktop desktop.js desktop Active window + snapshot 25 /voice voice.js voice Command parse + audio transcription 26 /logs logs.js — Log viewer All pages handle loading (skeleton), error (retry button), empty (icon + message), and data (full UI) states. All pages clean up on destroy. Core JS Infrastructure File Purpose js/app.js App initialization, 27 route registrations, sidebar/shortcut inits, store wiring js/router.js Hash-based SPA router with lifecycle hooks, lazy page loading, active nav tracking, object-export fix js/api.js REST client + WebSocket manager with auto-reconnection js/state.js Observable store with localStorage persistence (subscribe, set, get) js/template-loader.js HTML template fetcher with {{variable}} , {{{raw}}} , {{#if}} , {{#each}} js/components/Sidebar.js Static HTML sidebar with data-action event dispatch for nav, collapse, theme toggle CSS Design System 5 CSS files, dark-first glassmorphism theme: File Contents css/globals.css Design tokens (colors, spacing, z-index, shadows), CSS reset, glass utilities css/layout.css Grid layout, sidebar, header, page-content areas css/components.css ~20 reusable component styles (tables, modals, toasts, forms, cards, tabs, etc.) css/themes.css Dark theme variables, theme utility classes css/animations.css Transitions, skeleton loading, slide-in/out, fade Terminal Implementation Detail The terminal page ( /terminal ) runs all logic server-side: _cwd is a module-level variable in handlers/terminal.py , initialized to os.path.expanduser("~") Every safe_subprocess_run() call passes cwd=_cwd , so cd has actual persistent effect Every response includes "cwd": _cwd — the JS client reads this to update its prompt display GET /api/v1/terminal/cwd provides the initial directory on page mount Autocomplete: POST /api/v1/terminal/autocomplete returns suggestions from a COMMAND_REFERENCE (~90+ commands, 14 categories) JS is a thin input/output relay — no client-side cd tracking Sidebar Implementation The sidebar is static HTML (not a JS component) to avoid stale store subscriptions that caused the Sidebar.js component to accumulate duplicate listeners. Navigation uses data-action attributes dispatched through a global document event listener. The [data-action="toggle-sidebar"] handler is a single listener on the document, not on individual elements — this was the root cause fix for the sidebar toggle bug. Running ./scripts/fiona-pages-start # default port 8765 ./scripts/fiona-pages-start --port 8080 # custom port ./scripts/fiona-pages-start --debug # verbose logging Open http://fiona.agent:8765 in a browser. Status All 27 pages fully implemented. Production concerns (authentication, HTTPS) still being addressed. This is a supplementary control surface — the CLI and Tkinter GUI remain the primary Fiona interfaces.
docs.title: Web Dashboard (fionaLocalPages)

docs.text: PhiConnect PhiConnect is Fiona's standalone encrypted computer-to-computer chat app. It is separate from fiona edit , like Vsee, and uses CamComs crypto for chat messages instead of remote-control instructions. Open The App python3 -m fiona.cli phiconnect If Fiona is installed into the active environment: fiona phiconnect What It Does creates a local PhiConnect identity under ~/.config/fiona/phiconnect/ shows your public key in the Settings tab lets you trust another computer's public key starts a local encrypted chat receiver on port 5000 by default sends chat messages to a peer host and port encrypts every sent message with the peer public key decrypts received messages with the local private key records inbound and outbound chat events in ~/.config/fiona/phiconnect/chat.log displays messages from the last 3 minutes and refreshes the view every 5 seconds Local Loopback For same-machine testing: Open PhiConnect. In Settings, use Use Local Public Key . Start the receiver. Send to 127.0.0.1 on port 5000 . This tests the real encryption/decryption path without needing a second computer. Two Computer Setup On each computer: Open PhiConnect. Export or copy the local public key from Settings. Trust the other computer's public key. Set the peer host to the other computer's LAN IP. Use port 5000 , unless the peer is listening on a different port. Start both receivers, then send messages. Common Send Errors Missing peer public key: outbound encryption has no recipient key yet. Connection refused: no PhiConnect receiver is listening on the target host/port. Signature/trust failure: the inbound sender public key is not trusted or does not match the sender identity. Agent Bridge PhiConnect provides two optional agent integration modules under PhiConnect/ : ForemanChatHandler foreman_handler.py defines ForemanChatHandler , a GUI-facing handler that wraps ForemanAgent for multi-agent orchestration. It provides a toggle ( foreman_enabled ) to switch between simple single-agent chat ( AgentChatHandler ) and full orchestration via ForemanAgent . When the Agent bridge checkbox in the PhiConnect GUI is enabled, chat messages sent through the GUI are forwarded to the local Ollama agent for processing. The ForemanChatHandler class handles the orchestration pipeline: Simple queries (greetings, chit-chat) are routed through the lightweight AgentChatHandler to avoid unnecessary token usage. Complex tasks are decomposed and executed by ForemanAgent using a multi-step orchestration pipeline with planning, sub-agent delegation, and result synthesis. A CancellationToken is passed throughout to support cancellation. from PhiConnect.foreman_handler import ForemanChatHandler handler = ForemanChatHandler(chat_store=store) handler.foreman_enabled = True handler.send_message( session_id="...", message="What is the capital of France?", token=CancellationToken(), on_message=lambda role, content: print(role, content), on_error=lambda err: print("Error:", err), on_complete=lambda: print("Done"), ) PhiConnectAgentBridge bridge.py defines PhiConnectAgentBridge , a lightweight bridge that intercepts inbound chat messages and automatically replies via the local Ollama agent. When the bridge is active, every inbound message (that does not already start with [Fiona] ) is forwarded to the Ollama client, and the agent's response is sent back as a chat reply. from PhiConnect.bridge import PhiConnectAgentBridge bridge = PhiConnectAgentBridge(config) bridge.handle_message(event) # forwards to Ollama and sends reply Both modules require the Agent subsystem and an active Ollama instance on http://localhost:11434 .
docs.title: PhiConnect

docs.text: QuikTieper QuikTieper is Fiona's local access layer. It listens for simultaneous global key chords and runs configured actions. Config Default config path: ~/.config/fiona/bindings.json Basic Commands python3 -m fiona.cli init python3 -m fiona.cli list python3 -m fiona.cli run python3 -m fiona.cli edit Installed-command equivalents: fiona init fiona list fiona run fiona edit Explicit layer commands: python3 -m fiona.cli quiktieper list python3 -m fiona.cli quiktieper run python3 -m fiona.cli quiktieper edit App Import QuikTieper can discover installed Linux .desktop launchers and import them into the bindings file. python3 -m fiona.cli import-apps --dry-run python3 -m fiona.cli import-apps Filtering behavior: skips obvious games, demos, helpers, uninstallers, and many settings panels skips most K... apps by default keeps practical KDE tools such as Konsole, Kate, KWrite, KCalc, KDE Connect, KDE System Settings, Kdenlive, KDevelop, KMail, KOrganizer, KRDC, and KSystemLog Override flags: python3 -m fiona.cli import-apps --include-all-k-apps python3 -m fiona.cli import-apps --include-low-value-apps Key Assignment Generated app launch chords use alt plus three distinct safer letters and avoid duplicate pressed-key sets. python3 -m fiona.cli assign-keys --dry-run python3 -m fiona.cli assign-keys python3 -m fiona.cli assign-keys --reassign Command Normalization normalize-app-cmds applies curated command presets for common workstation apps. python3 -m fiona.cli normalize-app-cmds --dry-run python3 -m fiona.cli normalize-app-cmds Capabilities app launching from chord bindings installed desktop application discovery app-specific shortcuts gated by active-window matching shell command execution pointer movement and click helpers allowlisted remote actions for CamComs instructions structured macro instructions with nested steps Example Bindings alt + b + r + v opens Brave alt + v + s + c opens VS Code alt + t + e + r opens terminal

docs.text: RecallVault RecallVault is Fiona's persistent "remembrance" store — a key-value database with categories, tags, TTL-based expiry, import/export, timestamped backups, and optional TF-IDF semantic search. Purpose Long-term Memory : Store facts, configuration snippets, or notes that persist across sessions. Categorization & Tagging : Group information into categories and attach multiple tags for rich filtering. TTL-based Expiry : Entries can auto-expire after a configurable number of seconds. Portability : Import and export entries as JSON or CSV files. Safety : Automatic timestamped backups before any destructive write. Semantic Search : Pure-Python TF-IDF index ranks results by relevance (no external dependencies). Commands Save a remembrance fiona recall remember "phi-host" "192.168.1.10" --category network fiona recall remember "db-password" "s3cret" --tags secret,prod --ttl-seconds 86400 fiona recall remember "theme" "dark" --category gui --tags ui,preferences Options: --category (default: "general"), --tags (comma-separated list), --ttl-seconds (auto-expire after N seconds). Search remembrances fiona recall search "phi" # Searches key, value, category, AND tags fiona recall search # List all entries List categories fiona recall categories Statistics fiona recall stats Returns 12 fields: total entries, per-category and per-tag counts, storage size (bytes + human-readable), oldest/newest entry, expired/tagged/untagged counts, categories list. Export fiona recall export --format json # Pretty-printed JSON array fiona recall export --format csv # CSV with headers: key,value,category,tags,created_at,ttl_seconds fiona recall export --output /path/to/export.json Import fiona recall import /path/to/entries.json # Auto-detects format from extension fiona recall import /path/to/entries.csv JSON format: list of objects with key , value , optional category , tags , ttl_seconds . CSV format: headers key,value,category,tags,created_at,ttl_seconds . Imported entries with existing keys overwrite the stored value. Backup fiona recall backup # Creates recallvault-{timestamp}.json Forget an entry fiona recall forget "phi-host" Clear the vault fiona recall clear API Endpoints Method Endpoint Description GET /api/v1/recall/search?q=... Search entries (optional query) POST /api/v1/recall/remember Save/replace entry ( key , value , category? , ttl_seconds? , tags? ) DELETE /api/v1/recall/forget/{key} Remove entry by key GET /api/v1/recall/stats Vault statistics (12-field object) TUI Integration The Recall page in the fiona cli TUI displays the most recent snippets from the vault and updates in real-time. Storage Data is stored as a JSON array of objects in: ~/.config/fiona/recallvault.json Backups are written to ~/.config/fiona/recallvault-{YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS}.json . Python API from RecallVault import ( DEFAULT_RECALL_PATH, RecallEntry, remember, # Save/replace an entry (key, value, category?, ttl_seconds?, tags?) search_recall, # Search by key/value/category/tags forget, # Remove entry by key list_categories, # List unique categories clear_recall, # Delete the vault file load_recall, # Load all entries (auto-purges expired) recall_stats, # Get 12-field analytics dictionary export_recall, # Export vault to JSON or CSV file import_recall, # Import entries from JSON or CSV file (merges) backup_recall, # Create timestamped backup copy SemanticIndex, # TF-IDF semantic search index ) RecallEntry dataclass fields Field Type Default Description key str (required) Unique entry identifier value str (required) Stored value category str "general" Single category label tags list[str] \| None None Optional list of tags ttl_seconds int \| None None Time-to-live in seconds ( None = no expiry) created_at str (auto) ISO-8601 creation timestamp accessed_at str "" Last access timestamp (updated on search) remember(key, value, *, category, ttl_seconds, tags, path) -> Path Saves or replaces an entry. If ttl_seconds is set, the entry will be auto-purged on the next load_recall() call after that many seconds have elapsed since created_at . load_recall(path) -> list[RecallEntry] Loads all entries, filtering out any that have exceeded their TTL. Expired entries are silently purged and the file is rewritten. recall_stats(path) -> dict Returns: - total_entries , per_category , per_tag - total_storage_bytes , total_storage_human - oldest_entry (key + created_at), newest_entry - expired_entries (count that would be purged by TTL) - tagged_entries , untagged_entries - categories export_recall(path, output, fmt) -> Path fmt="json" — write as pretty-printed JSON array fmt="csv" — write as CSV with headers If output is None, generates recallvault-export-{timestamp}.{fmt} import_recall(path, input, fmt) -> int Auto-detects format from file extension if fmt is None Merges with existing entries (same key = overwrite) Returns count of imported entries backup_recall(path) -> Path Copies the vault file to recallvault-{YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS}.json in the same directory. Creates an empty backup if the vault doesn't exist. SemanticIndex (TF-IDF search) Pure-Python TF-IDF implementation with zero external dependencies: from RecallVault import SemanticIndex index = SemanticIndex() index.build(entries) # Build TF-IDF matrix from entry dicts results = index.search("query", limit=5) # Returns [{key, score}, ...] sorted by relevance index.add_entry("key", "text") # Marks index for rebuild Tokenizes by word boundaries, lowercases Computes term frequency / inverse document frequency Scores by cosine similarity of TF-IDF vectors Gracefully degrades (empty results) if no documents indexed

docs.text: SciPhi — Scientific Operating System SciPhi is a scientific investigation and simulation engine for Fiona. It is built around the Opsim (Optimized Simulation) Kernel — a reasoning engine that plans, constructs, executes, validates, and explains scientific investigations. It behaves like a lead scientist coordinating a research team, not like a function that computes equations. SciPhi is not a general-purpose chatbot or a simple calculator. It is a structured scientific computation platform designed for reproducibility, transparency, and rigor. Architecture User Request → Opsim Kernel → Scientific Planner → Scientific Model → Problem Compiler → Solver Selection Engine → Solver → Validation → Uncertainty Analysis → Report Generation The system is split into three independent layers: Layer Responsibility Never knows about Models Equations, variables, parameters, assumptions Solvers, computation Solvers Numerical methods, convergence criteria Science, domains Kernel Planning, compiling, selecting, validating Neither science nor numerics directly Opsim Kernel ( SciPhi/kernel/ ) The central orchestrator that owns the full investigation lifecycle: Module Component Responsibility opsim.py OpsimKernel Orchestrator — runs the full pipeline advisor.py SimulationAdvisor Checks if simulation is needed (or if analytical solution exists) planner.py ScientificPlanner Determines domain, governing equations, mathematical form compiler.py ProblemCompiler Translates scientific model → computational problem solver_selector.py SolverSelectionEngine Matches problem form to solver capabilities evaluator.py ScientificEvaluator Unit consistency, conservation laws, physical bounds uncertainty.py UncertaintyAnalyzer Error propagation, confidence estimates hypothesis.py HypothesisEngine Generates and evaluates testable hypotheses provenance.py ProvenanceTracker Structured record of every scientific decision report.py ReportGenerator Compiles all phases into structured investigation report Scientific Models ( SciPhi/models/ ) Models describe only the science — equations, variables, parameters, assumptions, constraints, and domain-specific constants. They never reference solvers or numerical methods. Domain Models Form Physics Kinematics, Dynamics (harmonic oscillator), Thermodynamics (ideal gas), Electromagnetism (Coulomb/RC circuits), Quantum (particle in a box) ALGEBRAIC, ODE_INITIAL_VALUE, PDE, HYBRID Chemistry Reaction kinetics (Arrhenius), Equilibrium (van't Hoff), Stoichiometry (mass/mole/yield) ODE_INITIAL_VALUE, ALGEBRAIC Biology Logistic growth (population dynamics), SIR epidemic model ODE_INITIAL_VALUE Earth Science Energy balance climate model (zero-dimensional) HYBRID Engineering Structural analysis (beam bending/buckling), Circuit analysis (RC/RL) ALGEBRAIC, HYBRID Total: 13 models across 5 domains . All models implement the ScientificModel abstract base class from SciPhi.interfaces.model . Solvers ( SciPhi/solvers/ ) Solvers describe only the computation — numerical methods, convergence criteria, and algorithmic parameters. They never reference scientific domains. Solver Methods Forms ODESolver RK4, Euler, DOPRI5 (adaptive) ODE initial value problems AlgebraicSolver Newton-Raphson, Bisection, Fixed-point Algebraic systems MonteCarloSolver Standard MC, Importance sampling Stochastic problems SymbolicSolver SymPy solve/simplify/dsolve Symbolic mathematics Optimizer Gradient descent, Nelder-Mead, BFGS Optimization problems All solvers implement the Solver ABC from SciPhi.interfaces.solver and declare capabilities for automatic selection. CLI Usage # Full scientific investigation (Opsim pipeline) fiona sciphi research "What is the trajectory of a projectile launched at 45 degrees?" # Run a specific model directly with custom parameters fiona sciphi simulate KinematicsModel --params '{"initial_angle": 45, "initial_velocity": 10}' # Validate a simulation result against known data fiona sciphi validate result.json # List available models and solvers fiona sciphi list-models fiona sciphi list-models --domain physics fiona sciphi list-solvers Visualization SciPhi includes an optional visualization module ( SciPhi.visualization ) for plotting simulation results: plot_result(result) — time-series, bar, or heatmap plots from SimulationResult data plot_report(report) — multi-panel figure with results, validation status, and uncertainty plot_comparison(results, labels) — overlay multiple scenarios All functions use dark theme styling and return base64 data URLs or save to file. Requires matplotlib (optional dependency). Provenance Every investigation produces a complete provenance record containing: Models used and their versions Governing equations and constants Solver selection and configuration Assumptions and approximations Validation results Uncertainty estimates Full decision traceability Test Status 322 tests pass, 6 skipped (sympy optional dependency).
docs.title: SciPhi — Scientific Operating System

docs.text: SeeOnDesk SeeOnDesk is Fiona's first desktop-awareness subsystem. It identifies the active app/window from desktop metadata. Screen capture is implemented. Visual recognition via an ML classifier is not yet integrated. Commands Show the currently focused app/window: python3 -m fiona.cli seeondesk active Show a fuller desktop-awareness snapshot: python3 -m fiona.cli seeondesk status Installed-command equivalents: fiona seeondesk active fiona seeondesk status Detection Backends SeeOnDesk currently: prefers kdotool for KDE/Wayland active-window detection falls back to xdotool and xprop on X11-compatible sessions reports backend: unavailable when it cannot access the desktop session Output Depending on the backend and session permissions, output can include: success/failure state backend used active window id app class inferred app name window title process id process name session type current desktop name Process Tracking ProcessTracker iterates /proc/*/comm to find processes by name substring, with no psutil dependency required. It registers and triggers watcher callbacks when matching processes are detected. fiona seeondesk status # includes process tracking output Workspace Awareness WorkspaceWatcher polls workspace state via kdotool (with wmctrl fallback), detects active workspace changes, fires change callbacks, and tracks the current workspace ID. Action Discovery Discover available actions from system state: fiona --discover-actions Prints a categorized list of actions grouped by process, workspace, and window context, with action name, description, and confirmation requirements. Screen Capture The vision.py module captures the full screen or a specific window to disk. It prioritizes KDE's spectacle on Wayland, falls back to grim for generic Wayland, and uses scrot or gnome-screenshot on X11. Screen captures are used by the Agent's seeondesk_analyze action for vision-based QA via the local Ollama model. from SeeOnDesk.vision import capture_screen, capture_window, analyze_screen capture_screen("/tmp/screenshot.png") analyze_screen("What application is visible?") Remaining Work SeeOnDesk is the base for richer context: ML classifier for visual recognition app-specific action contexts agent integration

docs.text: TerminalAssist TerminalAssist is Fiona Terminal Assistance ( fAT ). It provides a high-fidelity, btop-inspired terminal dashboard, a real-time sliding command center, and a system status API. Commands Open the sliding command center: fiona cli # or fiona fat tui Preview the command center without entering curses mode: fiona cli --preview Show the redesigned dashboard: fiona fat status Print machine-readable system status (JSON API): fiona api # or shortcut fiona fat api Dashboard The dashboard is a high-density system monitor inspired by btop . It features live resource tracking and project environment checks. Live metrics include: - System : Hostname, Kernel, Uptime, User, OS, Architecture. - CPU : Model, Usage %, Per-core usage, Speed, and Temperature. - Memory : RAM (Used/Total) and Swap usage. - Disk : Root partition usage and I/O activity (Read/Write). - Network : IP address, Interface, Traffic (RX/TX), and Signal strength (if Wi-Fi). - GPU : Utilization and Temperature (NVIDIA/Intel/AMD). - Power : Battery percentage and charging state (for laptops). - Security : Firewall status and available system updates. Real-Time Command Center The command center is a fullscreen curses UI with a non-blocking 1-second auto-refresh engine. Controls: left/right or h/l: slide pages up/down or k/j: select action /: live search across all actions enter: run selected action q or Esc: quit (or clear search) Quick Actions Page The TUI includes a Quick Actions page that detects your current Desktop Environment (KDE, GNOME, XFCE) and provides session controls: - Lock Screen - Logout - Suspend - Reboot - Shutdown Search and Navigation Press / to enter search mode. As you type, the TUI filters all available actions across all pages in real-time. Dynamic Pages The TUI includes dynamic data-driven pages: - History : Displays the 10 most recent actions from the CmdTrace log. - Recall : Displays the 10 most recent snippets from the RecallVault . Output Capture One-shot commands load in an internal output panel. Output controls: up/down: scroll line by line page up/page down or space: jump enter, backspace, q, or Esc: return to the command center Interactive or long-running actions (marked with ↗ ) such as btop or GUI apps launch externally in the foreground. Management Page The TUI includes a Management page for quick access to system tools: - System Monitor (btop) : Direct shortcut to launch btop. - Status dashboard : Text-based system summary. - Host status : Detailed host service checks. - Open editor : Fiona GUI configuration editor. Zellij Workspace fAT can generate and launch a multi-pane Zellij workspace: fiona fat run Current Limits no automatic service control from the dashboard yet fiona fat run requires Zellij to be installed and should be launched intentionally from a real terminal
docs.title: TerminalAssist

docs.text: Voice Voice is Fiona's speech interface subsystem. It provides wake-word detection, push-to-talk, and audio/visual feedback. Wake Word Engine The WakeWordEngine detects a spoken wake word and triggers registered callbacks. Supported backends (auto-detected in priority order): Backend Library Quality Picovoice pvporcupine Best (offline, fast) Snowboy snowboy Good MyCroft Precise mycroft_precise Good Gracefully degrades if no backend is installed — logs a warning and allows manual trigger via push-to-talk. Push-to-Talk The PushToTalk class implements a global hotkey listener using pynput . Default hotkey: Ctrl+Space Fires on_press and on_release callbacks Gracefully disables itself if pynput is not available Feedback Engine The FeedbackEngine provides three feedback channels: Channel Mechanism Fallback Audio Plays .wav / .mp3 / .ogg via aplay or paplay Silent if no sound file Desktop notification notify-send with urgency levels Silent if unavailable Status bar Text output (GUI status area) Always available Convenience methods: feedback.acknowledge() # "Listening..." sound + notification feedback.success(msg) # Success sound + notification feedback.error(msg) # Error sound + critical notification Sound files are stored in ~/.config/fiona/sounds/ . GUI Tab The Voice tab in the shared editor ( fiona edit ) provides: Voice Control section: - Wake word engine status (Available / Unavailable) - Start/Stop listening toggle - Wake word text entry (default "fiona") - Manual trigger button ("Hey Fiona") Feedback section: - Test sound buttons (Ack, Error, Success) - Test Notification button - Urgency selector (low / normal / critical) Push to Talk section: - Status indicator (Available / Unavailable) - Hotkey display (Ctrl+Space) - Start/Stop listener toggle CLI Commands fiona voice wake-test # Test wake word detection fiona voice feedback-test # Test audio/notification feedback Dependencies Optional runtime dependencies (not required for base installation): pip install pvporcupine # Best wake word detection pip install snowboy # Alternative wake word detection # pynput is a core dependency (already installed) Graceful Degradation All Voice components follow Fiona's graceful degradation pattern: If no wake word library is installed → engine reports "Unavailable", works via manual trigger only If pynput is missing → push-to-talk reports "Unavailable" If no sound files exist → play_sound() returns False without error If notify-send is missing → notifications silently fail

docs.text: Vsee Vsee is Fiona's current holography software layer. It is a point/edge 3D wireframe viewer, not true optical holography yet. Open The App python3 -m fiona.cli vsee With specific input files: python3 -m fiona.cli vsee --points ./points.csv --edges ./edges.csv The Vsee process is a foreground GUI app and stays open until the window is closed. Data Model Points are rows with an id and 3D coordinates: id,x,y,z A,-1,-1,-1 B,1,-1,-1 Edges connect point ids: source,target A,B Capabilities load point and edge CSV files edit point and edge tables in the GUI validate duplicate point IDs validate missing edge references project 3D coordinates into a 2D canvas using numpy parse editable table data using pandas render connected wireframe shapes adjust rotation and scale controls Current Limits Vsee is currently a coordinate viewer. Saved scenes, richer primitives, camera presets, animation, live data binding, and real projection/hologram output are future work.
